import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4831103662799812215'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_function-call-13739633365081319055'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Process Books
book_records = []
for b in books_data:
    b_id_str = b.get('book_id', '')
    details = b.get('details', '')
    
    # Extract ID
    # Assumes format "bookid_123"
    match_id = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    book_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    # Extract Year
    # Look for 4 digit year 19xx or 20xx
    # Many descriptions start with "Published ... on Month DD, YYYY"
    # match = re.search(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', details)
    # Better: find all, and check context? 
    # Actually, the first 4-digit number starting with 19/20 is likely the year.
    # ISBNs might contain it but usually don't appear as isolated 4 digit words first.
    
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if not years:
        year = None
    else:
        # Heuristic: The publication year is usually the first year mentioned
        year = int(years[0])
        
    if year:
        decade = f"{str(year)[:3]}0s"
        book_records.append({'id': book_id, 'decade': decade})

df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_records)

# Process Reviews
review_records = []
for r in reviews_data:
    p_id_str = r.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating = r.get('rating')
    
    if rating is None:
        continue
        
    try:
        rating = float(rating)
    except:
        continue
        
    # Extract ID
    match_id = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', p_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    p_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    review_records.append({'id': p_id, 'rating': rating})

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(review_records)

# Merge
# Inner join to keep only reviews for books we have decade info for
df_merged = pd.merge(df_reviews, df_books, on='id', how='inner')

# Aggregate
# Group by decade
stats = df_merged.groupby('decade').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('id', 'nunique')
).reset_index()

# Filter
filtered_stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10].sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False)

print("__RESULT__:")
print(filtered_stats.to_json(orient='records'))

import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4831103662799812215'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_function-call-13739633365081319055'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Process Books
book_records = []
for b in books_data:
    b_id_str = b.get('book_id', '')
    details = b.get('details', '')
    
    # Extract ID
    # Assumes format "bookid_123"
    match_id = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    book_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    # Extract Year
    # Look for 4 digit year 19xx or 20xx
    # Many descriptions start with "Published ... on Month DD, YYYY"
    # match = re.search(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', details)
    # Better: find all, and check context? 
    # Actually, the first 4-digit number starting with 19/20 is likely the year.
    # ISBNs might contain it but usually don't appear as isolated 4 digit words first.
    
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if not years:
        year = None
    else:
        # Heuristic: The publication year is usually the first year mentioned
        year = int(years[0])
        
    if year:
        decade = f"{str(year)[:3]}0s"
        book_records.append({'id': book_id, 'decade': decade})

df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_records)

# Process Reviews
review_records = []
for r in reviews_data:
    p_id_str = r.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating = r.get('rating')
    
    if rating is None:
        continue
        
    try:
        rating = float(rating)
    except:
        continue
        
    # Extract ID
    match_id = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', p_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    p_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    review_records.append({'id': p_id, 'rating': rating})

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(review_records)

# Merge
# Inner join to keep only reviews for books we have decade info for
df_merged = pd.merge(df_reviews, df_books, on='id', how='inner')

# Aggregate
# Group by decade
stats = df_merged.groupby('decade').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('id', 'nunique')
).reset_index()

# Filter
filtered_stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10].sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False)

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4831103662799812215'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_function-call-13739633365081319055'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Process Books
book_records = []
for b in books_data:
    b_id_str = b.get('book_id', '')
    details = b.get('details', '')
    
    match_id = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    book_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    # Extract Year
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    if not years:
        year = None
    else:
        year = int(years[0])
        
    if year:
        decade = f"{str(year)[:3]}0s"
        book_records.append({'id': book_id, 'decade': decade})

df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_records)
print("Books count:", len(df_books))
if not df_books.empty:
    print("Books sample:", df_books.head().to_dict(orient='records'))

# Process Reviews
review_records = []
for r in reviews_data:
    p_id_str = r.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating_str = r.get('rating')
    
    if rating_str is None:
        continue
        
    try:
        rating = float(rating_str)
    except:
        continue
        
    match_id = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', p_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    p_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    review_records.append({'id': p_id, 'rating': rating})

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(review_records)
print("Reviews count:", len(df_reviews))
if not df_reviews.empty:
    print("Reviews sample:", df_reviews.head().to_dict(orient='records'))

# Check overlap
if not df_books.empty and not df_reviews.empty:
    common_ids = set(df_books['id']).intersection(set(df_reviews['id']))
    print("Common IDs count:", len(common_ids))
    
    # Merge
    df_merged = pd.merge(df_reviews, df_books, on='id', how='inner')
    print("Merged rows:", len(df_merged))
    
    # Group by decade
    stats = df_merged.groupby('decade').agg(
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
        distinct_books=('id', 'nunique')
    ).reset_index()
    
    print("Stats by decade:")
    print(stats.to_string())

    # Filter
    filtered_stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10].sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False)
    print("Filtered stats:")
    print(filtered_stats.to_string())
    
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print(filtered_stats.to_json(orient='records'))
else:
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print("[]")

I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4831103662799812215'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_function-call-13739633365081319055'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Process Books
book_records = []
for b in books_data:
    b_id_str = b.get('book_id', '')
    details = b.get('details', '')
    
    match_id = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    book_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    # Extract Year
    # Find all 4-digit numbers
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    
    # Filter valid years (1900-2023)
    valid_years = []
    for y in years:
        if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023:
            valid_years.append(int(y))
    
    if not valid_years:
        continue
        
    # Heuristic: First valid year is likely the publication year
    year = valid_years[0]
    
    decade = f"{str(year)[:3]}0s"
    book_records.append({'id': book_id, 'decade': decade})

df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_records)

# Process Reviews
review_records = []
for r in reviews_data:
    p_id_str = r.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating_str = r.get('rating')
    
    if rating_str is None:
        continue
        
    try:
        rating = float(rating_str)
    except:
        continue
        
    match_id = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', p_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    p_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    review_records.append({'id': p_id, 'rating': rating})

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(review_records)

# Merge
if not df_books.empty and not df_reviews.empty:
    df_merged = pd.merge(df_reviews, df_books, on='id', how='inner')
    
    # Group by decade
    # Calculate average rating of all reviews in the decade
    # And count distinct books
    stats = df_merged.groupby('decade').agg(
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
        distinct_books=('id', 'nunique')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter
    filtered_stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10].sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    if not filtered_stats.empty:
        best_decade = filtered_stats.iloc[0]['decade']
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps(best_decade))
    else:
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps("No decade met the criteria"))
else:
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print(json.dumps("No data found"))

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4831103662799812215'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_function-call-13739633365081319055'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Process Books
book_records = []
for b in books_data:
    b_id_str = b.get('book_id', '')
    details = b.get('details', '')
    
    match_id = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    book_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    # Extract Year
    # Find all 4-digit numbers
    years = re.findall(r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b', details)
    
    # Filter valid years (1900-2023)
    valid_years = []
    for y in years:
        if 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023:
            valid_years.append(int(y))
    
    if not valid_years:
        continue
        
    # Heuristic: First valid year is likely the publication year
    year = valid_years[0]
    
    decade = f"{str(year)[:3]}0s"
    book_records.append({'id': book_id, 'decade': decade})

df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_records)

# Process Reviews
review_records = []
for r in reviews_data:
    p_id_str = r.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating_str = r.get('rating')
    
    if rating_str is None:
        continue
        
    try:
        rating = float(rating_str)
    except:
        continue
        
    match_id = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', p_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    p_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    review_records.append({'id': p_id, 'rating': rating})

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(review_records)

# Merge
if not df_books.empty and not df_reviews.empty:
    df_merged = pd.merge(df_reviews, df_books, on='id', how='inner')
    
    # Group by decade
    # Calculate average rating of all reviews in the decade
    # And count distinct books
    stats = df_merged.groupby('decade').agg(
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
        distinct_books=('id', 'nunique')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter
    filtered_stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10].sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    if not filtered_stats.empty:
        best_decade = filtered_stats.iloc[0]['decade']
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps(best_decade))
    else:
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps("No decade met the criteria"))
else:
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print(json.dumps("No data found"))

Brooklyn?

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4831103662799812215'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_function-call-13739633365081319055'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Process Books
book_records = []
for b in books_data:
    b_id_str = b.get('book_id', '')
    details = b.get('details', '')
    
    match_id = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    book_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    # Extract Year
    years = re.findall(r'\d{4}', details)
    valid_years = [int(y) for y in years if y.startswith(('19', '20')) and 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023]
    
    if not valid_years:
        continue
        
    year = valid_years[0]
    decade = f"{str(year)[:3]}0s"
    book_records.append({'id': book_id, 'decade': decade})

df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_records)

# Process Reviews
review_records = []
for r in reviews_data:
    p_id_str = r.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating_str = r.get('rating')
    
    if rating_str is None:
        continue
        
    try:
        rating = float(rating_str)
    except:
        continue
        
    match_id = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', p_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    p_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    review_records.append({'id': p_id, 'rating': rating})

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(review_records)

# Merge
if not df_books.empty and not df_reviews.empty:
    df_merged = pd.merge(df_reviews, df_books, on='id', how='inner')
    
    # Aggregate
    stats = df_merged.groupby('decade').agg(
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
        distinct_books=('id', 'nunique')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter
    filtered_stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10].sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    if not filtered_stats.empty:
        best_decade = filtered_stats.iloc[0]['decade']
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps(best_decade))
    else:
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps("No decade met criteria"))
else:
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print(json.dumps("No overlap found"))

import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load data
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4831103662799812215'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(locals()['var_function-call-13739633365081319055'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Process Books
book_records = []
for b in books_data:
    b_id_str = b.get('book_id', '')
    details = b.get('details', '')
    
    match_id = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    book_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    # Extract Year
    years = re.findall(r'\d{4}', details)
    valid_years = [int(y) for y in years if y.startswith(('19', '20')) and 1900 <= int(y) <= 2023]
    
    if not valid_years:
        continue
        
    year = valid_years[0]
    decade = f"{str(year)[:3]}0s"
    book_records.append({'id': book_id, 'decade': decade})

df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_records)

# Process Reviews
review_records = []
for r in reviews_data:
    p_id_str = r.get('purchase_id', '')
    rating_str = r.get('rating')
    
    if rating_str is None:
        continue
        
    try:
        rating = float(rating_str)
    except:
        continue
        
    match_id = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', p_id_str)
    if not match_id:
        continue
    p_id = int(match_id.group(1))
    
    review_records.append({'id': p_id, 'rating': rating})

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(review_records)

# Merge
if not df_books.empty and not df_reviews.empty:
    df_merged = pd.merge(df_reviews, df_books, on='id', how='inner')
    
    # Aggregate
    stats = df_merged.groupby('decade').agg(
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
        distinct_books=('id', 'nunique')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter
    filtered_stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10].sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    if not filtered_stats.empty:
        best_decade = filtered_stats.iloc[0]['decade']
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps(best_decade))
    else:
        print("__RESULT__:")
        print(json.dumps("No decade met criteria"))
else:
    print("__RESULT__:")
    print(json.dumps("No overlap found"))

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10203571539406728710.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10203571539406728710.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
